scenario: Orion Retail handles 12000 orders per day in Camunda 8.8 inside a single embedded subprocess. Whenever a cancelOrder message arrives while the subprocess is active, the team must run a refund branch in parallel with the existing work without cancelling the main flow of the subprocess.
question: Which BPMN construct triggers a parallel refund branch inside the same subprocess when the cancelOrder message arrives?

options.text: A non-interrupting event subprocess started by a message start event runs the refund branch in parallel with the main flow of the parent subprocess
An interrupting event subprocess started by a message start event runs the refund branch and cancels the main flow of the parent subprocess
A boundary message event on the embedded subprocess starts the refund branch and cancels the main flow of the parent subprocess
An ad-hoc subprocess started by the cancelOrder message arrival runs the refund branch in parallel without affecting the main flow

a.text: Correct. Non-interrupting event subprocesses run a side flow within the parent scope without cancelling the main flow, which is exactly the parallel refund requirement.

b.text: Incorrect. Interrupting event subprocesses cancel the main flow on trigger; the scenario requires the main flow to continue.

c.text: Incorrect. Boundary events attach to a host activity; a non-interrupting boundary would also need to be placed on the subprocess, but the documented internal pattern is the event subprocess.

d.text: Incorrect. Ad-hoc subprocesses execute internal activities in any order without a fixed flow; they are not triggered by external messages.

explanation: Non-interrupting event subprocesses started by a message start event run as a side flow inside the parent subprocess without cancelling the main flow.
